hints.Pater: 'Pater' is a noun meaning 'father' — it is the subject
hints.caelis: 'caelis' is a noun in the ablative plural meaning 'heavens'

hints.to eat: 'to eat' is 'edere' or 'esse' (different verb) — this 'es' comes from 'esse' (to be)

hints.Imperfect: Imperfect of 'esse' 2nd person would be 'eras' — 'es' is the present form
hints.Perfect: Perfect of 'esse' 2nd person would be 'fuisti' — 'es' is the present form
hints.Future: Future of 'esse' 2nd person would be 'eris' — 'es' is the present form

hints.eo, ire, ii, itum: These are the principal parts of 'ire' (to go)
hints.do, dare, dedi, datum: These are the principal parts of 'dare' (to give)
hints.sto, stare, steti, statum: These are the principal parts of 'stare' (to stand)

hints.Genitive: Genitive of 'pater' would be 'patris' — 'Pater' is the nominative form
hints.Vocative: While 'Pater' could be vocative (used in address), here it also serves as the subject of the clause
hints.Ablative: Ablative of 'pater' would be 'patre' — 'Pater' is the nominative form

hints.1st declension: 1st declension nouns have genitive in -ae — 'patris' ends in -is
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension nouns have genitive in -i — 'patris' ends in -is
hints.4th declension: 4th declension nouns have genitive in -ūs — 'patris' ends in -is

hints.Noun: 'noster' modifies 'Pater' — it is not a noun but an adjective showing possession
hints.Adverb: Adverbs modify verbs — 'noster' modifies the noun 'Pater'
hints.Conjunction: Conjunctions join words or clauses — 'noster' shows possession

hints.Interrogative pronoun: 'qui' can be interrogative in questions, but here it introduces a relative clause modifying 'Pater'
hints.Indefinite pronoun: The indefinite 'qui' means 'anyone' — here 'qui' introduces a relative clause
hints.Personal pronoun: Personal pronouns are 'ego', 'tu', 'is' — 'qui' is a relative pronoun

hints.Nominative: Nominative plural of 'caelum' would be 'caela' — 'caelis' is dative or ablative plural
hints.Dative: While 'caelis' could be dative plural, after 'in' it serves as ablative of place
hints.Accusative: Accusative plural of 'caelum' would be 'caelos' — 'caelis' has the -is ablative plural ending

hints.1st declension: 1st declension nouns have genitive in -ae — 'caeli' ends in -i
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension nouns have genitive in -is — 'caeli' ends in -i
hints.4th declension: 4th declension nouns have genitive in -ūs — 'caeli' ends in -i

translation: Our Father who art in heaven
